title: Seth B. Watts, PhD
text:
About
  
  Assistant Professor of Criminal Justice & Criminology at Texas State University.

title: Seth B. Watts
text:
Assistant Professor · Texas State University

Studying how technology and innovation shape policing, public safety, and community health.
I examine how emerging tools — from AI-assisted body-worn cameras to co-response models — reshape police behavior, officer decision-making, and community outcomes. My work sits at the intersection of criminology, public health, and applied policy evaluation.

Policing Technology & AI Body-Worn Cameras De-escalation Public Health Program Evaluation

title: Seth B. Watts
text:
Recent Work
All publications →

title: Seth B. Watts, PhD
text:
Research
My work centers on policing, technological innovation, and public health — three domains that increasingly converge as law enforcement agencies adopt new tools and face growing demands for behavioral change.
I study how emerging technologies reshape day-to-day police practice. A significant portion of my research examines body-worn cameras: how automated AI-assisted review changes supervisor behavior, officer accountability, and officer acceptance of monitoring systems. I use field experiments, quasi-experimental designs, and large-scale administrative records to identify causal mechanisms rather than correlations.
A second thread of my research addresses de-escalation training — what it is, what works, and why. I use systematic reviews and meta-analytic methods alongside original evaluations to synthesize evidence across programs and jurisdictions. This line of work has direct policy relevance as departments face pressure to reduce use-of-force incidents and improve officer-community relationships.
My public health work examines police responses to opioid overdoses and co-response models, focusing on how structural arrangements between law enforcement and public health agencies shape outcomes for individuals in crisis.

title: Seth B. Watts, PhD
text:
Approach
I work at the intersection of criminology, sociology, and public health — borrowing methods from each. My preferred designs involve randomized experiments, difference-in-differences, and regression discontinuity when possible. When the data demand it, I rely on survey methods and administrative record linkage. I use R and Stata for analysis.
I am committed to open science practices: pre-registering studies, posting replication code, and sharing findings through policy briefs and practitioner engagement.
